text: The patient is positioned in the lithotomy position, and surgical drapes are applied. The operating table is adjusted to a Trendelenburg position.

The uterus is suspended using two Prolene sutures for improved exposure. The surgeon retracts the sigmoid colon and makes a peritoneal incision at the promontory. Medial dissection is extended towards the recto-uterine pouch, facilitating access to the anterior rectal wall and posterior vaginal vault.

The surgeon suspends the uterus using two proline sutures for improved exposure. The sigmoid colon is retracted, and a peritoneal incision is made at the promontorium. Medial dissection is extended towards the recto-uterine pouch, allowing access to the anterior rectal wall and posterior vaginal vault.
The surgeon performs sharp dissection on the lateral side of the rectum, encountering thicker parorectal tissue, likely due to chronic prolapse.
The surgeon continues sharp dissection on the lateral side, revealing thicker parorectal tissue, likely due to chronic prolapse.

The surgeon performs dissection close to the rectal fascia to avoid injury to the hypogastric nerves, ensuring complete mobilization of the rectum. The dissection continues until the levator muscles are visible, preparing for proper mesh positioning.

The surgeon performs dissection close to the rectal fascia, carefully avoiding the hypogastric nerves. The dissection continues circumferentially around the rectum to achieve complete mobilization, ensuring proper positioning of the mesh.
The surgeon continues the dissection, mobilizing the rectum until the levator muscles become visible, indicating progress in the rectal prolapse procedure.

A trimmed two-strip purlin mesh is prepared outside the surgical field. The first strip is fixed to the anterior rectal wall using PDS sutures. The second strip is then positioned and secured to the posterior wall of the vagina, completing a posterior colpopexy.
A trimmed two-strip purlin mesh is prepared outside the surgical field. The first strip of the mesh is then fixed to the anterior rectal wall using PDS sutures.
The surgeon brings up the second strip of the mesh and secures it to the posterior wall of the vagina, performing a posterior colpopexy.
The surgeon closes the peritoneum with continuous absorbable sutures, completing the rectopexy procedure without complications.
The surgeon closes the peritoneum with continuous absorbable sutures, completing the procedure without complications.
